Pharos — Adverse Drug Reaction Signal Detection

Disproportionality Analysis of OpenFDA FAERS Reports

Author

Pharos Pipeline · OpenFDA FAERS data

Published

May 18, 2026

Introduction

Pharmacovigilance is the science of monitoring drugs for safety problems after they reach the market. Spontaneous reporting systems — where healthcare professionals and patients submit voluntary reports of suspected adverse reactions — are the primary data source for post-market surveillance. The US FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS), served via the OpenFDA API, is the largest such database, containing millions of reports submitted since 1969.

This report applies disproportionality analysis to a sample of FAERS data. The core question is: does a given drug appear alongside a given adverse reaction more often than you would expect by chance, given how commonly that reaction is reported for all other drugs? When the answer is yes — and the excess is statistically robust — that drug-reaction pair is flagged as a safety signal warranting further investigation. A signal is not a proven causal link; it is a statistical anomaly that prompts closer scrutiny.

Methods

Data source

Adverse event reports were fetched from the OpenFDA FAERS endpoint (api.fda.gov/drug/event.json) using the Pharos ingestion pipeline. Each report was exploded into one row per reaction, normalised to lowercase, deduplicated by safety report ID + reaction, and stored in a local SQLite database.

Data summary 
summary = pd.DataFrame({
    "Item": ["Drugs analysed", "Total AE report-rows", "Drug-reaction pairs evaluated", "Signals flagged"],
    "Value": [n_drugs, f"{n_ae_total:,}", f"{n_pairs:,}", n_flagged],
})
summary.style.hide(axis="index").set_properties(**{"text-align": "left"})
Item Value
Drugs analysed 3
Total AE report-rows 37,153
Drug-reaction pairs evaluated 2,387
Signals flagged 375

Reporting Odds Ratio (ROR)

For each drug X and reaction Y, a 2×2 contingency table is constructed:

Reaction Y Other reactions
Drug X a b
All other drugs c d

\[\text{ROR} = \frac{a/b}{c/d}\]

The 95% confidence interval uses the log-normal approximation:

\[\text{SE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b} + \frac{1}{c} + \frac{1}{d}}\]

\[\text{CI} = \exp\!\left(\ln(\text{ROR}) \pm 1.96 \cdot \text{SE}\right)\]

Signal threshold: ROR lower 95% CI > 1 (strict).

Proportional Reporting Ratio (PRR)

\[\text{PRR} = \frac{a\,/\,(a+b)}{c\,/\,(c+d)}\]

The associated chi-squared statistic uses the exact Pearson formula with no Yates’ continuity correction:

\[\chi^2 = \frac{N \cdot (ad - bc)^2}{(a+b)(c+d)(a+c)(b+d)}\]

Signal thresholds: PRR ≥ 2 and n ≥ 3 and χ² ≥ 4.

Zero-cell handling

Pairs where any contingency cell equals zero are excluded from analysis. Zero cells make the log-normal SE undefined for ROR and produce degenerate PRR values. A Haldane correction (+0.5 to all cells) would recover these pairs but introduces bias; exclusion is the more conservative choice for MVP.

Results

Flagged signals

A drug-reaction pair is flagged when all four criteria are simultaneously met: ROR lower CI > 1, PRR ≥ 2, n ≥ 3, χ² ≥ 4.

ROR distribution

The histogram below shows the distribution of ROR values across all 2387 evaluable drug-reaction pairs. Most pairs cluster near 1.0 (no disproportionality); the long right tail contains the flagged signals.

Top reactions per drug

The charts below show the ten most frequently reported adverse reactions for each drug in the current dataset (ranked by raw report count, not by signal strength).

Limitations

  • Voluntary reporting bias. FAERS data is submitted spontaneously — serious, novel, or widely-publicised reactions are over-reported relative to their true incidence. Disproportionality signals reflect the reporting rate, not the underlying pharmacological risk.

  • MedDRA term inconsistency. Adverse reaction terms are entered as free text and normalised to MedDRA preferred terms by OpenFDA. The same clinical event may appear under multiple terms (e.g. “bleeding” vs “haemorrhage”), leading to undercounting. No MedDRA synonym mapping has been applied in this analysis.

  • No causality inference. A flagged signal indicates that a drug-reaction pair is reported more often than expected given the background rate. It does not establish that the drug caused the reaction. Confounding by indication, co-medications, and patient demographics are not controlled for.

  • Small background population. The current database contains reports for only 3 drugs (Ibuprofen, Aspirin, Metformin). Disproportionality estimates are most reliable when the background population is large and diverse. Signals computed against a narrow background are statistically noisier and should be interpreted cautiously.

Reproducibility

This report was generated on 18 May 2026 from data stored in db/pharos.db. All signals are recomputed at render time from the raw adverse_events table — no pre-cached values are used.

To regenerate:

# 1. Refresh the database (fetches latest OpenFDA data)
python pipeline/run_refresh.py

# 2. Render this report
quarto render analysis/report.qmd

Software versions (captured at render time):

import importlib, platform
